Q1: I tried to keep things fairly consistent, usually studying in the evenings after my part-time job and other commitments. I'd block out about two hours most days, maybe a bit more on weekends. Being a visual learner, I'd often create diagrams and charts from the lecture materials, which took time but helped things stick. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access good internet at the library or campus.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, but honestly it varied quite a bit depending on what was due. Early in the semester I was more consistent, but as assignments piled up, I'd shift my schedule around. Deadlines definitely dictated when I'd put in extra hours. I tried to maintain my 12 hours a week, but some weeks it was front-loaded, others back-loaded.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check if new lecture videos were posted or to see announcements about upcoming deadlines. The gamified tasks they introduced were actually kind of engaging, so sometimes I'd log in just to see my progress on those. I also checked the discussion boards fairly regularly since talking with peers helped me understand concepts better when I couldn't afford tutoring or extra textbooks.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became automatic to log in first thing when I sat down to study. The gamified elements helped with that honestly—seeing points accumulate or badges unlock gave me small wins even when motivation was low. It became part of my wind-down routine too, checking discussions before bed. The habit formed naturally once I realized how much content was being posted regularly.
Q5: The lecture videos were essential for me—I'd watch them multiple times and pause to sketch out visual notes. The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful because other students would explain things differently than the professor, which sometimes clicked better. The gamified tasks made reviewing material less tedious. Visual diagrams and infographics posted by instructors were gold for my learning style.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped the lengthy reading PDFs because I couldn't always print them and reading on screen for hours hurt my eyes. Some of the supplementary articles felt redundant if the video already covered the topic well. I also didn't engage much with the practice quizzes early on—probably should have, given my assignment scores weren't great.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see direct relevance to exams or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. The gamified tasks were clever because they made even mundane review feel like I was accomplishing something. If something was too text-heavy without visual elements, I'd often feel like I was wasting time even if the content was important.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford time-wise, even though some were genuinely interesting. Required ones got priority obviously, but I noticed I engaged more deeply with optional content when it was gamified or had some social element like peer discussions. Without that extra hook, optional stuff fell to the bottom of my list when life got busy.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download videos and watch them offline when my internet at home was unreliable. I filled notebooks with hand-drawn diagrams and concept maps that I'd study from constantly. Sometimes I'd screenshot slides and organize them on my phone to review during bus rides. A lot of my actual learning happened with these physical materials spread across my desk.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd spend hours working through problems in my notebook, connecting concepts visually, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also study with classmates in person—we'd meet at the library and work through difficult material together. The LMS would just show I hadn't logged in, but I was genuinely engaged with the course content.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video, watch it once online, then rewatch it two or three more times offline while taking detailed visual notes. Same with slides—I'd access them once to download, then spend hours with printed copies or screenshots. My actual engagement with materials was way higher than my access logs would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when I was feeling pretty down. My mood wasn't great—things felt heavy for a while, and even though my stress and anxiety weren't overwhelming, this persistent sadness made it hard to care about coursework. Financial pressures from having low resources added to it. I'd still log in but felt disconnected.
Q13: Fatigue was a real issue because I was juggling work and extracurriculars alongside six online courses. Some weeks I'd access the LMS but just scroll without really absorbing anything. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid it rather than tackle it head-on. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS often got my tired, end-of-day attention rather than my best focus.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered elsewhere, thinking about money troubles or just feeling sad. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. Sometimes I'd open materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't actually learning. Those sessions probably looked fine in the data but were pretty hollow.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even knowing they mattered—my completion rate wasn't great despite understanding the material well enough for exams. Part of it was that low motivation dragging on me; starting felt harder than it should have. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more up to it, but that better day didn't always come. The sadness made initiating tasks feel exhausting.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation when internal drive was lacking. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly my LMS activity would spike. Without that pressure, I'd drift. The gamified tasks helped somewhat because they created mini-deadlines and goals, but major assignments still got pushed until the last stretch. My engagement was definitely deadline-clustered.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback on exams motivated me to keep my study strategies going, which is probably why my exam scores stayed strong. But when assignment feedback was critical, it sometimes made me feel worse and I'd avoid similar tasks. With my mood already low, negative feedback hit harder than it probably should have.
Q18: The course structure with regular video releases gave me a rhythm to follow, which helped. Having six courses meant I needed that predictability. The gamified elements added structure too—knowing there were points to earn kept me checking in. But when structure was loose or self-directed, I struggled more because my own motivation wasn't reliable enough to fill that gap.
Q19: Visual content made engagement way easier for me—well-designed slides, video demonstrations, diagrams. The gamification genuinely helped my engagement even when I felt low. What made it harder was dense text-based content and assignments that required resources I didn't have easy access to. Also, when due dates clustered together across my six courses, everything became overwhelming.
Q20: I can relate to that pattern somewhat. Sometimes you're exploring, trying to understand, watching videos repeatedly, participating in discussions—but the actual assignment feels like a mountain you can't climb. The LMS activity might show genuine effort to learn, but converting that into submitted work requires a different kind of energy. When you're struggling emotionally, that final push is hardest.
Q21: That's basically me with exams versus assignments. My exam scores were strong because I studied effectively offline with my visual methods and peer discussions. But my LMS activity and assignment completion didn't reflect that understanding. You can learn deeply through methods that don't register in the system. Performance and logged activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they access, how often they participate in discussions. That's useful for spotting who's present. But it completely misses the quality of that engagement, offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone's actually learning or just going through motions. It's like measuring attendance without measuring attention.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm actively thinking, making connections, creating those visual maps that help concepts stick. It's participating in discussions because I'm genuinely curious, not just for points. Real engagement is mental investment, and that doesn't always align with clicking through an LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was dealing with persistent sadness throughout the course, or that my strong exam performance came from hours of offline visual studying. It wouldn't capture the peer study sessions, the notebooks full of diagrams, or the financial stress affecting my energy. It definitely wouldn't show that the gamified tasks were sometimes the only thing getting me to engage on my harder days.
